data.title: Genuine Parts: Long-Term Upside Still Here, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
While the market waits for a genuine sales growth inflection, we see further opportunities for Genuine Parts' (NYSE:GPC) equity story. The company just released its Q4 results, and we will report a few positive takeaways to consider.

Since our double-down, GPC's stock price has increased by more than 10% (Fig 1). Regarding our buy rating status, we backed the company for the following reasons: 1) GPC's valuation compared to peers, 2) supportive capital allocation priorities, and 3) a story of solid execution. Additionally, on a higher level, we continue to report a positive correlation between increased car complexity & an aging car population in auto parts companies' sales evolution.

Fig 1

Q4 Results and Our Positive Take

GPC top-line sales increased by 3.3% to $5.8 billion compared to last year (Fig 2). The company's Q4 headlines were slightly better than Wall Street market expectations; however, GPC's 2025 outlook is around the market's bar, and does not include details to its pension plan, which could push its EPS lower. The company's sales benefitted from the acquisition and a favorable FX. Additionally, an extra selling day contributed 110 basis points to its 3.3% sales growth, which we believe likely contributed to the gross margin leverage in Q4. Gross profit reached $2.1 billion; a 1.8% increase compared to Q4 2023.

By division, GPC's Auto segment EBITDA margin was down 100 basis points year-on-year to 7.8%, and GPC's Industrial segment EBITDA margin decreased by 40 basis points to 12.9%. Moving forward, it will be crucial for Genuine Parts to focus on execution to ensure it does not need to lower its outlook. Going down to the P&L analysis, GPC's adjusted net income reached $224 million, with a $1.61 EPS. In the adjustment EPS, the company does not report restructuring costs, which will continue to be reported as a non-recurring expense also in 2025.

On the balance sheet, the company's inventory increased by 17.9% year over year. The company reached an operating cash flow of $1.3 billion, which was insufficient to cover investing activities and CAPEX, $1.1 billion and $567 million invested, respectively. For this reason, GPC's debt is slightly higher than the 2023 results. That said, the company has a strong capital structure with a total debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.2x.

Fig 2

Why are we positive?

  1. On a higher level, looking at the automotive companies' readouts, we report that Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY) is back to invest in new Internal Combustion Engine [ICE] models for €800 million. Even if GPC and P911 are not directly correlated, this is a positive sign of car complexity. There is some positive news that the EU EV adoption rate is significantly slowing down; we believe it will be an ICE sale ban by 2035. We very much like the BMW CEO approach that "raised concerns about the risks tied to the EU's reliance on Chinese EV battery manufacturers and stressed the importance of a more flexible regulatory approach that embraces multiple low-emission technologies;"
  2. Looking at the peers' readouts on a US development, we report Advance Auto's announcement to withdraw from several markets. This is positive for the other players in the space. The company is moving from Washington, California, Oregon, Arizona, and Nevada and will close some underperforming stores in different markets. This will reduce the number of stores located in the city by almost 700. We believe that as commercial customers are informed of the closure of their local Advance store, these clients will look to shift their business to supply products. GPC with NAPA might really benefit from this announcement. According to our calculation, Advance Auto's average sales stores produced approximately $1 million in sales. Napa has approximately 12% sales of its USA stores in these states, and assuming 20/30% of higher market share penetration, this could imply $175 million of additional sales;
  3. In the last quarter, GPC's gross margin increased by ~49 basis points to 36.9%, with the company's adjusted SG&A deleveraged by ~210 basis points. This translates into a higher core operating profit of $172 million. Looking ahead, the company still projects an annualized saving of $200 million per year thanks to its internal restructuring target. This will be viewed positively by Wall Street;
  4. Still related to GPC capital priorities, we must report a 3% DPS increase. "The company has paid a cash dividend every year since going public in 1948, and 2025 marks the 69th consecutive year of increased dividends paid to shareholders."

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last assessment, we arrived at a 2024 turnover estimate of $23.2 billion. The company's sales reached $23.4 billion. Looking to 2025, we see the company taking a conservative stance on the top line, with an H2-weighted recovery in both segments. So, we implied a 2% sales growth in both segments thanks to acquisitions/and the Advance Auto announcement and considering FX pressure. Assuming a gross margin evolution aligned with Q4 results, intangible asset amortization up by low-single digits, and restructuring expense of $165 million, this would translate into a core operating profit of 6.6%, with a decline of 20 basis points compared to 2024 results. With a 24% tax rate, our net income reached $1.1 billion with an EPS of $8.1. The company guides an adjusted EPS between $7.75 - $8.25 (Fig 3) with approximately $0.30 EPS headwind on partially mitigating its pension plan.

Without looking at GPC's closest peers (O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY), Advance Auto Parts (AAP), and AutoZone (AZO)), we continue to value the company with an unchanged P/E multiple of 16x. Following a lower EPS projection, we lower the GPC target price from $132.8 to $129.6. We report that GPC has a double-digit EBITDA margin with an ROIC of above 15%. Despite a lower price, we confirmed our buy rating estimates on a twelve-month view. We also report that the PC P/E target is below its stock's historical averages (Fig 4) but warranted, in our view, given the lagged cyclical recovery in the industrial segment. We also note the core holder of the company is an equity income/dividend growth fund, which is most devoted to cash flow sustainability and dividend outlook. GPC scores highly in that regard, which is a positive sign.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Risks

We have covered GPC since 2022, so we report the following downside risks: 1) changes in consumer spending with a slower-than-anticipated 2025 industrial recovery (that said, with a Trump second mandate, GPC might benefit from industrial onshoring activities), 2) declines in the average age of vehicles or miles driven in the EU and USA, 3) higher competition with online channel shift, 4) failure in accretive acquisitions, 5) higher inventory as reported in Q4. On a new risk, we report FX changes and uncertainty on GPC's new pension plan.

Conclusion

We priced GPC based on a 12-month view and not on its long-term growth potential. Still, the company screens well on balance sheet ratio and dividend coverage. We are optimistic about the complexity of cars and the aging fleet in GPC's main markets. This is still a buy.

data.title: Tracking Seth Klarman's Baupost Group Holdings - Q4 2024 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Baupost Group's 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Klarman’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/13/2025. Please visit our Tracking Seth Klarman’s Baupost Group Holdings article for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the fund's moves during Q3 2024.

Baupost Group’s 13F portfolio value decreased from $3.52B to $3.43B this quarter. The total number of 13F securities increased from 21 to 22. The portfolio is heavily concentrated with Liberty Global, Willis Towers Watson, Alphabet, WESCO, and CRH plc together accounting for ~56% of the 13F holdings.

Since inception (1982), Baupost Group’s 13F portfolio has accounted for between 2.4% to 15% of the Assets Under Management (AUM). The current allocation is at the high-end of that range. The rest of the AUM is diversified among cash, debt, real estate, and hedges. On average, the fund has held ~25% cash over the last decade. Seth Klarman’s distinct investment style is elaborated in his 1991 book “Margin of Safety: Risk-averse value investing strategies for the thoughtful investor”. The book is out-of-print and copies sell for a huge premium.

New Stakes:

Ferguson Enterprises (FERG): FERG is a fairly large 5.72% of the portfolio position purchased this quarter at prices between $173.58 and $221.78. The stock currently trades at ~$182.

Restaurant Brands (QSR): The 5.51% QSR stake was established this quarter at prices between $64.91 and $72.84 and the stock is now just below that range at $63.19.

Sunrise Communications (SNRE): SNRE is a ~3% portfolio position that came about as a result of the spinoff from Liberty Global. It started trading in November 2024 at $47 per share, and it is now at $46.38.

Humana Inc. (HUM): The 2.56% HUM stake was established this quarter at prices between $233.08 and $303.12 and the stock currently trades at ~$266.

Genuine Parts (GPC), and Liberty Broadband (LBRDK): These very small (less than ~1% of the portfolio each) stakes were established this quarter.

Stake Disposals:

Fidelity National Information Services (FIS): The 2.31% FIS position was purchased during Q3 2022 at prices between ~$76 and ~$105. There was a ~250% stake increase during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$57 and ~$85. That was followed with a ~125% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$50 and ~$78. Q2 2023 also saw a ~40% increase at prices between ~$52 and ~$59. Q2 2024 saw a ~37% selling at prices between $66.89 and $78. That was followed by a ~72% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $72.48 and $85.04. The disposal this quarter was at prices between $80.14 and $90.57. The stock is now at $69.42.

Jacobs Solutions (J): Jacobs Solutions was a 1.31% of the portfolio stake established during H2 2023 at prices between ~$119 and ~$140. The stake was decreased by 30% in the next quarter at prices between ~$126 and ~$154. The last quarter saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$114 and ~$130. The elimination this quarter was at prices between $131.80 and $149.25. The stock currently trades at ~$130.

Fortrea Holdings (FTRE): The 1.30% FTRE position was primarily built during Q2 2024 at prices between $22.48 and $40.28. The last quarter saw a ~27% selling at prices between $19.48 and $27.92. The stake was sold this quarter at prices between $16.82 and $23.73. The stock currently trades at $15.39.

Stake Increases:

Willis Towers Watson (WTW): WTW is a large (top three) ~17% of the portfolio stake established in Q1 2021 at prices between ~$200 and ~$235. The two quarters through Q3 2021 saw a ~60% selling at prices between ~$202 and ~$270 while the next quarter there was a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$226 and ~$249. There was a similar reduction in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$191 and ~$243. Q3 2022 saw a ~40% increase at prices between ~$192 and ~$221 while during Q1 2023 there was a one-third reduction at prices between ~$220 and ~$257. Q3 2023 saw a ~72% stake increase at prices between ~$196 and ~$234. The stock currently trades at ~$326. The last few quarters also saw minor increases.

WESCO International (WCC): The ~6% WCC position was purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$133 and ~$194. The next quarter saw the stake built at prices between ~$152 and ~$191. There was a ~55% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$149 and ~$183. That was followed by a ~14%stake increase this quarter at prices between $163.76 and $212.73. The stock is now at ~$197.

CRH plc (CRH): The large (top five) ~7% CRH stake was primarily built during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$53 and ~$60. The stake was decreased by 19% during Q1 2024 at prices between $65.92 and $87.20. The next quarter saw a ~55% stake increase at prices between $72.72 and $86.02. The last quarter saw a ~38% reduction at prices between $71.76 and $93.24. The stock currently trades at ~$106. There was marginal increase this quarter.

Eagle Materials (EXP): EXP is a 4.59% of the portfolio stake established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$196 and ~$272. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$213 and ~$274. The last quarter saw a ~45% stake increase at prices between ~$212 and ~$292. That was followed by a ~28% stake increase this quarter at prices between $246.48 and $315. The stock currently trades at ~$240.

Herbalife (HLF): The 1.48% HLF position was increased by 34% during Q1 2024 at prices between $8.03 and $15.36. That was followed by a ~130% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between $7.65 and $11.70. This quarter saw another ~50% stake increase at prices between $6.33 and $8.67. The stock is now below their purchase price ranges at $5.62.

Note: They have a ~9% ownership stake in the business.

Stake Decreases:

Liberty Global (LBTYK): LBTYK is currently the largest position at ~17% of the portfolio. It was established in Q3 2018 at prices between ~$15 and ~$19 and increased by ~120% next quarter at prices between ~$10 and ~$18. There was another ~27% stake increase in Q1 2019 at prices between ~$10 and ~$16. Since then, the activity had been minor. There was a ~10% reduction in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$12 and ~$16. The two quarters through Q3 2023 saw another ~10% trimming. This quarter also saw a ~10% trimming. The stock currently trades at $11.54.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the Sunrise (SNRE) spinoff in November.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): The ~8% GOOG stake was purchased in Q1 2020 at prices between ~$53 and ~$76. The last three quarters of 2020 had seen a ~75% selling at prices between ~$55 and ~$92. There was a ~265% stake increase in Q1 2021 at prices between ~$86 and ~$105. Q4 2021 saw a ~22% trimming at prices between ~$133 and ~$151 while next quarter there was a ~7% increase. The zigzag trading pattern continued in the next quarter: ~30% reduction at prices between ~$106 and ~$144. That was followed with another ~60% selling during Q3 2022 at prices between ~$96 and ~$123. The stake was rebuilt next quarter at prices between ~$83.50 and ~$105. Q1 2023 saw a ~47% stake increase at prices between ~$87 and ~$109 while the next quarter saw a ~30% selling at prices between ~$104 and ~$128. There was another ~23% reduction during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$123 and ~$143. Q2 2024 also saw a ~65% selling at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. There was a ~37% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$150 and ~$192. The stock is now at ~$187. There was a minor ~3% trimming this quarter.

Clarivate Plc (CLVT): The 5.37% of the portfolio CLVT position was established during Q3 2023 at prices between $6.60 and $9.90. There was a ~13% stake increase in the next quarter. That was followed by a ~20% increase during Q1 2024 at prices between $7.01 and $9.44. The next quarter saw another ~27% stake increase at prices between $5.32 and $7.65. The stock currently trades below their purchase price ranges at $4.76. There was a minor ~7% trimming this quarter.

Note: They have a ~5.2% ownership stake in the business.

Dollar General (DG): DG is a 4.55% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$80 and ~$132 and the stock currently trades below that range at $75.63. There was a ~11% trimming this quarter at prices between $72.70 and $83.23.

Solventum Corp. (SOLV): The 2.78% SOLV stake established during Q2 2024 at prices between $50.75 and $70.06. There was a ~20% selling this quarter at prices between $66.05 and $75.07. The stock is now at $73.98.

Kept Steady:

Viasat (VSAT): VSAT is a ~3% of the portfolio position. Klarman first purchased VSAT in 2008 at a cost-basis in the high-teens. In July 2020, Baupost participated in Viasat’s 4.47M share private placement by acquiring ~2.56M shares at ~$39 per share. The last quarter saw a ~11% selling at prices between $11.94 and $24.49. The stock currently trades at $9.32.

Note: They have a ~10% ownership stake in the business.

GDS Holdings (GDS): The 2.94% GDS stake saw a ~65% stake increase during Q2 2024 at prices between $6.11 and $9.75. The stock currently trades at $43.01.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Klarman's 13F stock holdings in Q4 2024:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Baupost Group’s 13F filings for Q3 2024 and Q4 2024.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Mid-Teens EPS Growth Trumps The Risks
data.description:
The Health Care sector has been the worst of the 11 S&P 500 areas in the past 12 months, but we've seen some periods of modest relative strength in the last three months. Moreover, among small caps, Health Care is actually the best sector so far in 2025. Indeed, there has been some weak performance among the large-cap Health Care names since the middle of last year, including shares of UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) (NEOE:UNH:CA).

I was bullish on the stock throughout last year, and I reiterate a sanguine stance despite plenty of macro and fiscal uncertainties. The $482 billion market cap Managed Health Care industry company has dealt with firm-specific issues lately, too. We've seen the largest component of the Dow Jones Industrial Average struggle through the first half of the first quarter, widening the gap to fair value in my opinion.

What's more, the technical chart is now approaching key long-term support after breaching an area I noted in my previous analysis.

Health Care: Worst S&P 500 Sector YoY, But Best Among Small Caps YTD

Back in January, UNH reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $6.81 beat the Wall Street consensus forecast of $6.74 while revenue of $101 billion, up 6.8% from the same period a year earlier, was a significant $930 million miss. Shares fell 6% in the session that followed, making it back-to-back poor earnings reactions.

Ahead of the April report, the options market prices in a material 4.8% earnings-related stock price swing based on the straddle pricing-that's a full percentage point above the long-term implied move, so we could see another large reaction.

As for its Q4, UNH once again missed on the street's Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) estimate. The figure verified at 87.6%, which was also above UNH's own forecast. The firm's member mix, Medicaid reimbursements, and costs related to medications were drivers of the high metric. For reference, the MLR is like a health insurance company's profit margin, only inverted.

Still, the management team affirmed its FY 2025 revenue and earnings guidance in January; at the time, it saw $450-$455 billion on the top line and adjusted EPS to be in the range of $29.50-$30. The onus is now on UNH to continue to outperform its peers on execution and keeping costs in check.

Another weak spot was the margin story with OptumHealth, but other units picked up the slack. Overall, margins should be a bright spot this year, but a lot will hinge on policy changes, and the company hopes to improve on its FY 2024 MLR of 85.5%.

On the earnings outlook, it's steady as she goes in terms of operating EPS. The consensus calls for almost $30 of non-GAAP per-share earnings in the current year, with strong 12-14% of outyear through FY 2026 EPS gains. That makes today's mid-high teens price-to-earnings ratio compelling in my view.

The sellside has turned less upbeat, however, largely due to the tragedy in January and unease about what the Trump administration may do with healthcare policy and DOGE. But profitability trends are strong, evidenced by UNH's high 4.4% free cash flow yield.

UnitedHealth: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, now into FY 2025, if we assume operating EPS of $30.50 over the next 12 months and apply a 19x P/E, then shares should trade near $580.

I lowered my earnings multiple assumption by 1.6 figures due to potential action against major US health insurance companies in the new backdrop and after the January murder of Brian Thompson. That still leaves UNH materially undervalued, so I retain the buy rating.

UNH: Broadly Favorable Valuation Metrics

Key risks for UNH include higher MLRs industrywide if healthcare utilization increases, weaker growth in Optum, and the aforementioned policy uncertainty with RFK, Jr at the helm of the Department of Health and Human Services.

The company also exposed patients' data to hackers earlier this year, so cybersecurity challenges should be acknowledged.

Competitor Analysis

I also wanted to mention that seasonality is quite bullish for UNH shares right now. The March through July period has historically produced strong returns when analyzing trends over the past 10 years.

UNH: Bullish Calendar Trends

Looking ahead, corporate event risk data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, April 15 BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With the stock still undervalued in my estimation, UNH's technical situation has turned worse. Notice in the chart below that shares have fallen back below the key $555 mark I detailed last year. Now, with a high amount of volume by price right near where UNH trades, it is a battle between the bulls and the bears. The long-term 200-day moving average, while it is rising in its slope, is above price, which the longs never want to see.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph - it printed a bearish divergence to price when UNH notched a new all-time high in November. The good news is that with a steep drop just in the last few weeks, UNH is not far from major long-term support beginning at $475.

UNH: Shares Back Below Resistance, Long-Term Support in the Mid-$400s

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on UNH. Amid poor absolute and relative price action, the Health Care stalwart appears compelling on valuation ahead of a very bullish seasonal period.

data.title: Cryptocurrencies: Bitcoin Inches Down To 5-Week Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

This weekly update tracks some of the largest cryptocurrencies by market share: bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD). While both are considered to be high risk when it comes to investing, the two have foundational differences that investors should know.

We've also included XRP (XRP-USD), as it was one of the largest cryptocurrencies when this series began. According to Wikipedia, a cryptocurrency is "a digital asset designed to work as a medium of exchange that uses cryptography to secure its transactions, to control the creation of additional units, and to verify the transfer of assets."

Bitcoin

Bitcoin was the world's first cryptocurrency and decentralized digital currency. The first bitcoin transaction occurred in early 2009 and has since grown worldwide to a mainstream financial asset. It is often considered volatile, as seen in our first chart, but one can argue that it is also resilient.

Bitcoin's closing price edged lower, settling just above $95,500 - its lowest level in over five weeks. BTC remains up ~1% year to date, but is now ~10% below its record high.

Ether

Ether is a cryptocurrency run on the Ethereum blockchain platform and was launched in July 2015. It has the second-largest market share, despite being the newest of the three discussed in this article.

Ether's closing price ticked higher this week, but ETH remains down ~20% year to date and ~45% below its all-time high.

XRP

XRP, which is owned by Ripple, was launched in 2012 and was one of the larger cryptocurrencies for some time until new coins joined the market.

XRP surged during the last few months of 2024 and the during the first month of 2025. However, it pulled back in February and is now up only ~10% year to date.

Bitcoin vs. Ether vs. XRP

An index has been created in order to chart these three cryptocurrencies together, considering their significantly different pricing histories. The chart tells us which cryptocurrency's price has changed the most since November 9, 2017. At one point or another, all three have been at the top. At the time of writing, bitcoin is in the lead.

On January 10th, 2024, the SEC approved spot bitcoin ETFs from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC), ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB), Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB), Coinshares Valkyrie Bitcoin Fund (BRRR). Here are some of the quick takeaways from the spot bitcoin ETF launch.

On July 23rd, 2024, a handful of spot ether ETFs launched from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Ethereum Trust (ETHE), Franklin Ethereum ETF (EZET), Bitwise Ethereum ETF (ETHW), iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA), Fidelity Ethereum ETF (FETH).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Reinsurance Group: Baby Bonds Offer A Solid Return In Either Outcome
data.description:
Reinsurance Group of America Inc. (NYSE:RGA) has been hitting it out of the park. The largest life reinsurance company had an extremely challenging time with the pandemic. Its earnings contracted over 50% from 2019 to 2020 and were down over 80% from its 2017 numbers. But through that trough came higher reinsurance rates and, well, higher interest rates. The first ensured profitability in its base business, and the latter enhanced the yield earned on its float. In the most recent quarter, RGA demonstrated just how powerful these tailwinds were.

With over $22 per share of earnings, the company has more than tripled its pandemic year numbers. Estimates for the next 3 years look good, though RGA may be at the peak of the rate cycle and earnings are unlikely to grow much.

Outlook For RGA

RGA and other insurance stocks look relatively cheap if you focus on price to earnings ratios. Most of them are sporting a low P/E ratio and in RGA's case it is just about 8.5X. Historically, we have paid more attention to price to tangible book values as they are a better indicator pick trades in this sector. When this metric is high, it means that the company is in the midst of an upcycle in rates and investors are forgetting that rates come down eventually. When this metric is low, it means that despondency rules the day and one can look for a buying opportunity. Where are we today? Based on that logic, RGA looks actually expensive and would normally merit a sell rating from us at 1.22X tangible book value multiple.

But we have to adjust that based on one major factor. Where interest rates are headed. Our take here is that RGA and other reinsurance stocks will likely face lowered pricing going forward, and earnings have likely peaked here. On the other hand, the ultra-low price to tangible book values were more suited for the ZIRP (zero Interest rate policy) era. In that era, these stocks only made via their operating profits and did not make much on their float. You can see in the chart above that RGA held that 1.2X multiple on tangible book value from 2004-2007, when interest rates were generally higher than in the ZIRP era. Overall, we come away with a hold (versus a sell) and would generally consider buying this only at a sub 1.1X multiple of tangible book.

The Baby Bonds

Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated SB DB FX/FL56 (RZB) is something that we had on our watch list for some time, and we added them as low as $24.36. The company is rated A by S&P and junior/subordinated bonds here are rated BBB+. This may not seem like a slam dunk investment since it only has a coupon of 5.75%, but we added it as it was one of the highest quality picks for a short duration. This may seem contradictory to the bond's maturity date, which is in 2056. Well, there is more to this. But first, let us see how things work if the bond is redeemed. As long as you buy it under par, you should get more than 5.75% yield to call. At the $24.36 price point, the yield to call was well over 6.75%. This is pretty good for a sub two-year bond. iShares 1-5 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGSB) which focuses on quality at the low end of the duration curve, as an average yield to maturity of just 4.92%.

Plus, you make less than that after fees, and the average bond has a lower rating than this one. So this works get great for a call/redemption. What if the bond is reset on June 15, 2026?

The spread commencing on 6/15/2026 to the maturity date will be equal to three-month LIBOR, reset quarterly, plus 4.04%. The spread was set at a time when LIBOR was all we knew, but the real reset will obviously be adjusted to SOFR. So you will get SOFR+ 4.04% plus a small spread adjustment. The reason we like it is as part of a diversified portfolio is that you do want some shorter term rate exposure. If you have to have that (assuming they don't call it) you would want a really high spread for the quality of the bond. To give a comparative here, we can talk about Annaly Capital Management (NLY) which is a mortgage REIT with 6X leverage has Annaly Capital Management, Inc. PFD SER G (NLY.PR.G) which was issued with a LIBOR spread of just 4.172%. The credit quality of these firms is miles apart. So if you think 4.172% from NLY.PR.G is great, you should love 4.04% from RGA. Based on the market prices, (NLY.PR.G is trading over par), we think RZB is a fairly decent deal here. If our current financial conditions persist, it will be called/redeemed at $25.00 on June 15, 2026. If things get dicey in the markets, it is possible that redemption will be put off. In that case, we are happy picking up this spread relative to risk-free rates.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: Celcuity's High-Stakes Oncology Bet Is Underappreciated
data.description:
Celcuity (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:CELC) has spent almost all its resources developing gedatolisib, a pan-PI3K/mTOR inhibitor for advanced breast cancer. The company remains under the radar, with a valuation reflecting skepticism rather than potential despite being designated as a Breakthrough Therapy and demonstrating strong early efficacy data. With pivotal Phase 3 trials underway and a differentiated IV delivery mechanism to help alleviate toxicity concerns, the market may be misjudging Celcuity's risk-reward profile. Speculative plays have been punished in the current biotech environment, but I believe Celcuity's focused strategy may make it an overlooked winner later in 2025 and beyond.

Financials

The financials paint a picture of a company spending heavily to expand its pipeline - expected for a clinical-stage biotech company. Operational expenses rose to $30.1 million in Q3 2024 from $18.9 million in the prior year. This was largely due to an increase in R&D expenses to $27.6 million as the company ramped up clinical trial activity. That sort of spending can be a double-edged sword. On one hand, it indicates meaningful progress in late-stage trials - especially the VIKTORIA-1 and VIKTORIA-2 studies. But it also shows how heavily Celcuity relies on its cash position to fund operations - with no revenue stream to offset the burn.

As a buffer, cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments stood at $264.1 million at the end of Q3 2024. With $20.6 million of operating cash burn for the quarter, Celcuity theoretically has about 13 quarters of runway before it needs more funding if burn rates remain steady. But this timeline may shift with trial expenses, potential regulatory milestones, or strategic shifts. With strong results from VIKTORIA-1 and VIKTORIA-2, the company may be able to secure non-dilutive funding or a partnership rather than raising capital through equity - a trap often faced by small biotech firms.

The impact of a prolonged bear market for speculative biotechs is also not fully appreciated. Those that rely entirely on investor sentiment to fund their next phase of growth are often punished preemptively despite having healthy cash reserves. I believe this is representative of Celcuity's case. Due to this emotional reaction, the stock price does not currently capture all the strategic flexibility this balance sheet affords, especially when rivals developing PI3K inhibitors have had trouble due to safety issues and mixed efficacy. If Celcuity proves safer, it might be in a unique position to profit from an area of oncology that has seen several setbacks.

Clinical Pipeline

Even though Celcuity is focused on a single drug, its pipeline has depth. Gedatolib is its lead and only clinical asset developed for HR+ / HER2- advanced breast cancer, as well as additional exploratory work in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Its strategy is based on a simple, underappreciated premise: by simultaneously inhibiting PI3K and mTOR it may avoid some of the traps seen with other drugs in this space. Toxicity, including liver problems and severe hyperglycemia, has traditionally limited the use of PI3K inhibitors like alpelisib. In its intravenous formulation, Celcuity may have avoided first-pass metabolism in the liver.

VIKTORIA-1 and VIKTORIA-2 trials will determine if gedatolisib can capture meaningful market share. For patients progressing after CDK4/6 inhibitor treatment, VIKTORIA-1 is evaluating the drug together with fulvestrant with or without palbociclib. It is a very competitive setting, but the scarcity of compelling second-line options makes this a commercially viable option if gedatolisib shows durable responses. Probably the bigger bet is VIKTORIA-2, which is a first-line study using Gedatolisib in combination with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. If it succeeds, it could position the drug as an early-line treatment in combination with HR +/HER2- breast cancer, expanding the market even further.

I also believe that Celcuity's relatively small but important foray into prostate cancer is overlooked. An overlooked value driver could be the ongoing Phase 1b/2 trial in patients progressing on androgen receptor inhibitors but not chemotherapy. In prostate cancer, combination approaches have emerged, mainly targeting PI3K / AKT / mTOR pathway. Prostate cancer drugs with even modest efficacy may have very broad commercial prospects. The market generally shuns single-asset biotech companies with thin pipelines but, as Celcuity has demonstrated, one well-executed bet may be enough. The main question is not whether the drug works - early data suggest it does - but whether the company can demonstrate its differentiation in a way that regulators and prescribers see value in.

Targeting PI3K and mTOR

Complex signaling networks drive breast cancer, especially the HR +/HER2- subtype, to adapt to targeted treatments and survive. The most critical are PI3K and mTOR, which both regulate cell growth, metabolism, and survival. Mutations in PIK3CA, the key subunit of PI3K, are present in about 40% of HR+/HER2- breast cancers and are a well-known target. Past attempts to drug this pathway have had toxic results, especially with oral PI3K inhibitors that induce severe metabolic disturbances by interfering with insulin signaling. This has created a cautious regulatory environment, with the FDA increasing requirements for PI3K-targeted agents after several safety failures.

Celcuity's approach is unique because gedatolisib is not only a PI3K inhibitor but also an mTORC1/2 inhibitor, which may target resistance mechanisms that arise when only one component of the pathway is targeted. PI3K and mTOR are in the same signaling cascade, and inhibition of only PI3K can induce mTOR compensatory activation, reducing long-term efficacy of treatment. By targeting both, gedatolisib may provide more sustained tumor growth suppression in cancers that have developed resistance to standard endocrine therapy and CDK4/6 inhibitors. This dual mechanism is not only theoretically advantageous - early clinical data suggest that patients with and without PIK3CA mutations respond to the drug, suggesting that it may work independently of mutational status, unlike alpelisib, which is approved only for PIK3CA-mutant tumors.

Prostate cancer is an interesting new frontier for gedatolisib beyond breast cancer. The PI3K / AKT / mTOR axis also drives disease progression in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, especially in tumors resistant to androgen receptor inhibitors such as enzalutamide and abiraterone. The loss of the PI3K signaling-regulating tumor suppressor PTEN is observed in about 40% of prostate cancers, leading to hyperactivation of this pathway. This suggests that blocking PI3K and mTOR simultaneously may benefit tumors that have exhausted standard therapy options. Especially compelling is the fact that prostate cancer therapies historically have lacked novelty, which leaves a space in the market for novel targeted therapies. If it can show activity in this space, gedatolisib could present Celcurity with a secondary market opportunity that very few are paying attention to.

Encouraging Survival Data Strengthens Gedatolisib’s Case

In its latest press release, Celcuity reported overall survival data from its Phase 1b trial of gedatolisib in patients with HR +/HER2- advanced breast cancer that may be able to compete with existing therapies. Patients receiving gedatolisib in combination with palbociclib and endocrine therapy had a median OS of 77.3 months compared to 33.9 months for patients previously treated with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. These numbers are comparable to historical data for standard-of-care regimens and support Celcuity's Phase 3 trials.

Those findings are especially compelling because they align with Celcuity's strategy of blocking several resistance pathways at once. PI3K/mTOR inhibition in combination with CDK4/6 blockade and endocrine therapy appears to prolong survival beyond that observed with these treatments alone. Though there are always caveats to cross-trial comparisons, the extended OS in treatment-naive patients may suggest that gedatolisib may be effective earlier in settings important to the VIKTORIA-2 trial. We can expect weaker OS in pretreated patients who have progressed on standard therapy already; however, survival over 30 months is still noteworthy even in this population.

The significance of these findings remains to be fully appreciated by the market, probably due to lingering skepticism about PI3K inhibitors as a class. Still, Celcuity is positioning itself as the developer of a differentiated safety profile and the potential for broad efficacy beyond PIK3CA-mutated tumors. If the Phase 3 data repeats the early success, investor sentiment could change quickly.

Risks That Could Derail Celcuity’s Bet

The most immediate concern would be whether targeting PI3K and mTOR together will yield a meaningful clinical advantage without creating new toxicity problems. Whilst gedatolisib's intravenous delivery may limit liver-related side effects with oral PI3K inhibitors, its long-term safety profile is uncertain. The class of PI3K inhibitors has been characterized by problems beyond hepatotoxicity, including immune suppression and metabolic dysregulation. Even if delivered differently, patients may still experience serious adverse effects that prevent widespread adoption.

Another question is whether dual inhibition of PI3K and mTOR will be sufficient to prevent resistance mechanisms indefinitely. Tumors have a tendency to adapt - sometimes bypassing one signaling cascade by upregulating another. For example, the AKT pathway between PI3K and mTOR may still permit cancer cells to escape inhibition when not adequately suppressed. The question remains: is blocking both PI3K and mTOR sufficient to prevent compensatory resistance, or will tumors find other routes? Academic work has pointed out that targeting this pathway is complex and that even in preclinical models, combined inhibition induces resistance due to crosstalk with other survival pathways such as MAPK and JAK/STAT.

In this space in particular, I think competition is another underappreciated risk. Although CDK4 / 6 inhibitors are still the mainstays of HR + / HER2- breast cancer therapy, new-generation SERDs and AKT inhibitors are showing promise in clinical trials. Any of these emerging treatments with better efficacy or cleaner safety profiles might limit gedatolisib's market penetration even if it is approved. Investors should also consider the financial risk - Celcuity has a healthy cash position, but biotech burn rates can skyrocket. If trial timelines extend or unexpected costs occur, they may to another round of fundraising being required, causing dilution.

Valuation and Market Potential

Despite its massive spending on pre-commercial R&D, conventional valuation metrics fail to capture Celcuity's potential. Rather, the company is valued on its market potential for gedatolisib, particularly in the HR +/HER2- advanced breast cancer. In the U.S., the HR +/HER2- subtypes will account for about 70% of the approximately 310,720 new invasive breast cancer cases, or about 217,504 patients annually by 2024.

Concerning advanced stages, 6-10% of breast cancers are metastatic at diagnosis - de novo metastatic breast cancer. Averaging that across all of the HR + / HER2- population suggests an estimated 13,070 to 21,750 annual new de novo metastatic HR + / HER2- breast cancer cases.

The treatment costs for advanced breast cancer therapies are also quite high. For instance, Palbociclib (Ibrance) costs about $118,200 per year for patients. Assuming a similar price of gedatolisib, I would estimate that de novo metastatic patients could represent a US market size of approximately $1.55 billion to $2.5 billion annually.

This estimate excludes patients progressing to metastatic disease following first treatment or patients with recurrent disease, which may further expand the addressable market. Furthermore, with proven efficacy in other cancers such as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, gedatolisib may have expanded market potential.

Future Outlook

While the financials give Celcuity enough cash runway to get the pivotal trials through, the company largely relies on one asset. Due to this, any setback could be far greater than expected. Its valuation argument is more about market size than traditional revenue multiples - a size that would easily justify a much higher valuation if gedatolisib succeeds.

The market has not yet embraced gedatolisib's potential differentiation, likely due to skepticism about the PI3K pathway and the broader biotech downturn dampening sentiment for speculative names. Should Celcuity pull through on its upcoming readouts, current levels may prove to be a mispricing in retrospect. But I would emphasize that it is also an all-or-nothing proposition - the company is making a single bet that must pay off for shareholders to see the upside.

data.title: 10 Ideal 'Safer' January Dividend Buys, Out Of 40 Choices From Readers
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail, or comment to the author has been fair game for a reader favorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only rogues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Most valuable for the writer, however, are reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction. Examples include the reader who missed my “safer” dividend follow-up articles because they contain dividend payout ratios. The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha.

My ten-year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers.

Still, every month, readers have grumbled that they can’t find my ten Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

This year, SA has listed all the postings on my Dogcatcher investing group by date. So to find the summary and reference guide to each dog of the week portfolio, look at:

August 22, 2015 for I

October 13, 2016 for II

September 12, 2017 for III

September 13, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 12, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

Incidentally, My Ignition (DOTW IX) portfolio launched October 6, 2023, had all its selections in place by October 4, 2024, and will post its last return on October 3, 2025.

Last winter, a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M.

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the dogcatcher.

An observation from April 2023:

“Mr. Arnold, I enjoy reading your many articles and get some great ideas from them. However, I find the number of charts is a bit of overkill, many duplicating the info. I'm sure you have your set format and reasons why, but I'd be happy with only two charts; your sort by target gains and your sort by div yield. It would make it a much easier read. Thank You —pmbrandt 15Apr2023”

I’d gladly just submit those two charts and ditch all the verbiage. However, fifteen years ago, when I started submitting articles, the SA editorial team said they needed text to go with the graphics and topsy was born.

Still, there are complaints of too many words for little information.

A July 2024 prize comment was:

“anybody able to plough through this cruff and figuire which two stocks are buys in accordance with his title? these articles are borderline autistic.

Additionally, I do not think INTC is overbought. Its 14-day RSI of 48.11 indicates that the company is trading in a technically neutral state. The RSI is a stock indicator that ranges from 0 to 100, with overbought stocks being above 70 and oversold stocks being below 30.

—weitermachen 8/15/24.

I replied:

“@weitermachen Thanks for your comments! Just to clarify and crystallize the two suggested ideal dogs noted my July Dogs of the Dow article, they are the two Dow Index stocks showing dividends (from $1k invested) exceeding single share price: Verizon Communications, and Dow Inc.

As for the “overbought” three, I'm using an unsophisticated measure (unlike RSI) to back up my assertion. “Unbuyable” stocks is perhaps a better term for Goldman, JPMorgan, & Intel, whose dividend yields exceed their free-cash-flow yields. I have since employed terms ”cash rich” or “cash poor” to describe the status of ideal and not so ideal stocks.

Regards, FA.

This past month, I got in trouble with readers when I neglected to define an acronym used to describe the Ben Graham Value collection. Unintroduced, I dropped a GASV acronym on them which stood for “Graham All-Star Value” stocks, dogs, or pack.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, MoPays, and all my other articles, less obfuscating. I now describe the “safer” stocks as “cash rich” and the stocks whose dividends from $1K in vested do not equal their single share price as “cash poor.”

In June 2022 high-yields were alleged, by one reader, to be sure signs of Rogueishness. Not true; high-yields are sure signs of high-yield, how long the elevated yield lasts, depends on share-price and corporate directors. High-Beta is the most accurate measure of Rogueishness.

In February 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly paying dividend stocks:

“You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk.”

[See a summary of ten fair-priced December ReFa/Ro Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and five “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my “ideal” stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equals or exceeds share price. A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, recently spun off from Liberty media. A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Currently priced at $27.11, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 3.08%! A little over $1k investment now buys 37 shares, and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) of nearly 1.47 times the share price. Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 25 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in Nvidia Corp could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly, nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (Recently $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $3,215.43.)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: when the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1.000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 20.84% To 56.81% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By January 2025

Five of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this January 2025 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-28 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to January 2026 were:

TORM PLC (TRMD) was projected to net $568.13, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 77% less than the market as a whole.

Frontline PLC (FRO) was projected to net $566.68, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 99% less than the market as a whole.

Petrobras (PBR) was projected to net $544.34, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% over the market as a whole.

Park Hotels (PK) was projected to net $535.03, based on the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 100% over the market as a whole.

Golden Ocean Group (GOGL) was projected to net $441.44, based on the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 17% greater than the market as a whole.

Merck & Co (MRK) was projected to net $431.27 based on the median of target prices estimated by 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

Pfizer (PFE) was projected to net $279.46 based on the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 36% under the market as a whole.

Dow Inc (DOW) was projected to net $241.62, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 18% over the market as a whole.

Verizon (VZ) was projected to net $219.81, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 55% under the market as a whole.

Conagra Brands (CAG) was projected to net $208.35, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 72% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 40.36% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 22% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the “dog” moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as “dogs.” More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, “underdogs.”

The January 2025 ReFa/Ro By Analyst Targets

The January 2025 ReFa/Ro By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top January ReFa/Ro Stocks By Yield Ranged 7.66% To 38.81% Per YCharts

Top ten ReFa/Ro Dogs occupied five of eleven Morningstar sectors and also included 1 Closed End Investment Co (CEIC).

Three energy representatives placed first, second and ninth, Petrobras [1], TORM PLC [2], and Frontline PLC [9].

A lone real estate entity placed third, Park Hotels [3].

Three financial representatives placed fourth, sixth and eighth, Invesco Mortgage (IVR) [4], ARMOUR Residential (ARR) [6], and FS KKR Capital (FSK) [8].

A lone CEIC, placed fifth, Carlyle Credit (CCIF) [5]. Another loner, an industrial, Golden Ocean placed seventh (7), and, finally, one consumer staples member placed tenth. Altria Group (MO) [10], to complete the January ReFa/Ro Dogs by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top pie chart above shows the relative strengths of the top ten January ReFa/Ro dogs by dividend and price as of market close 2/17/2025.

This month, none of the top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs show a less than ideal status because all ten prices are lower than the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by all ten of the highest yield ReFa/Ro Dogs.

All ten lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding a single share prices.

34 ReFa/Ro Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 2/17/25

The lower chart reveals 34 ReFa/Ro Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the January ReFa/Ro was Petrobras at $2.58 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive was Johnson & Johnson at $31.48 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Seventeen of Forty ReFa/Ro Dogs Show No Dividends or Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Fifteen ReFa/Ro so infected are joined by two non-dividend payers: Petrobras, TORM PLC, Park Hotels, Invesco Mortgage, Carlyle Credit, Frontline, Dow, LyondellBasell, Pfizer, Enterprise Products, Canadian Utilities, Main Street Capital, Kenvue, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund, Reeves Utility Index Fund, are the fifteen. The two non-dividend payers (discovered by readers) were Banco BBVA Argentina, and Banco Macro. These seventeen are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember, this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, executive over-reach, and tariffs, too.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 16.3% To 49.97% Top Ten ReFa/Ro Upsides To January 2026; (32) Four -1.36% to -18.01% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a “market-sentiment” gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield “dog” metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 11.39% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 January ReFa/Ro Dogs As of 2/17/ 25

Ten-top ReFa/Ro dogs were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs, selected 2/17/25 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors plus one Closed End Investment Corporation.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield ReFa/Ro Dogs (34) To Deliver 36.53% Vs. (35) 32.79% Net Gains by All Ten Come January 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten ReFa/Ro Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 11.39% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The eighth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, TORM PLC, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 56.81%.

The five lowest-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs for January were: Carlyle Credit; Invesco Mortgage; Golden Ocean; Park Hotels; Petrobras, with prices ranging from $7.88 to $14.32.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of January were: Frontline; ARMOUR Residential; TORM; FS KKR Capital; Altria, with prices ranging from $17.67 to $53.29.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' “basic method” for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of “market sentiment” gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the post-election euphoria in the markets dies, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements will gain momentum into 2025 except the few tech stocks. Look for at least half of the ten ReFa/Ro Dogs to become fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly, with an assist from war clouds, or a pandemic of one sort or another.

Afterword

Buy 10 of 27 “Safer” January ReFa/Ro Dogs

January 2025, of the 27 Ideal Re/Fa/Ro were tagged “safer” for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. The ten “Ideal” candidates from the “safe” group showed dividend yields from $1,000 invested exceeding their single share prices.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Re/Fa/Ro Updates, out here, in public, away from the fire-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of “dividends” from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your “Safer” ReFa/Ro dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.
